name: Divya Gokulnath
title: Divya Gokulnath Was a Star Teacher. Then She Built a Classroom for Millions.
dek: How a teacher-turned-co-founder helped make learning go viral across India and beyond.
body_html:
Most kids dread the words "math class." Divya Gokulnath spent her career proving that maybe, just maybe, the problem was never the math, it was the way we were teaching it. As a co-founder of the edtech juggernaut BYJU'S, she helped turn the most groan-inducing subjects on earth into something millions of students would actually open an app to learn. That's not a small magic trick. That's borderline sorcery.

From the Front of the Class to the Front of a Movement

Divya's origin story starts where the best teachers' stories do: in the classroom, with a genuine gift for making hard things click. She was a student in the early test-prep sessions that would eventually grow into the company, then became a star teacher in her own right, the kind students request by name. That hands-on teaching experience became her superpower as a founder, because she understood the customer better than any spreadsheet ever could: she was the customer, on both sides of the desk.

Partnering to build BYJU'S, she brought something most tech founders simply don't have: real pedagogical instinct. She knew what made a lesson land, what made a kid lean in, and what made them quietly close the book in defeat.

The Creativity Was Making Learning Feel Like Play

Here's the genius of the BYJU'S approach. It didn't just film boring lectures and slap them online. The product leaned hard into visual storytelling, animation, and interactive lessons designed to make abstract concepts feel tactile and even fun. Geometry became something you could see move. The app turned studying into something closer to a game, with engaging characters and bite-sized progress that kept young learners coming back.

The breakthrough wasn't putting school on a screen. It was making the screen want to teach you.

The brand world was built on warmth and aspiration, positioning learning as joyful rather than punishing. The marketing was massive and emotional, leaning into the dreams of parents and students alike, and the company became one of the most recognizable names in its category through splashy sponsorships and ambassadors that put it everywhere you looked. Divya herself became one of the most visible women in tech, a relatable face who could speak the language of teachers and builders at the same time.

The Rise to Global Edtech Giant

The growth was staggering. BYJU'S rode a wave of demand to become one of the most valuable edtech companies in the world, reaching valuations reported in the billions at its peak and expanding its reach across countries through aggressive growth and acquisitions. Divya was regularly named among the most powerful and influential women in business, a self-made leader who built wealth and impact by betting that better-designed learning could scale to tens of millions of kids.

It's worth noting the company later faced well-documented financial and operational turbulence, the kind of dramatic highs and lows that big, fast-growing startups sometimes ride. But Divya's core contribution stands on its own: she helped prove that education could be a product people actually craved, designed with a teacher's empathy rather than an engineer's indifference.

She started by helping one room full of students understand. Then she handed that understanding to millions more.

She didn't disrupt the classroom. She just made the whole world want to sit in one.
